context.description: Reflective prose argues that symbolic weighting shapes perception more than facts, using flat Earth beliefs as an example. It links rejection of scientific order to inner symbolism and to figures such as Hodur, Dionysus, and Samael. The passage explains how adopting an alternative argument space can lead someone toward flat Earth thinking.
normalization_notes: Translated German prose into English summary.
Used symbolism_and_archetypes for mythic references and symbolic framing.

rows.paragraph_text: Symbolic weighting, not factual content, determines how the world is perceived.
rows.text_content: Symbolic weighting, not factual content, determines how the world is perceived.

rows.paragraph_text: Flat Earth arguments are presented as a case of rejecting scientific order by reshaping inner symbolism and reinterpreting evidence through an alternative argument space.
rows.text_content: Flat Earth arguments are presented as a case of rejecting scientific order by reshaping inner symbolism and reinterpreting evidence through an alternative argument space.

rows.paragraph_text: Mythic figures such as Hodur, Dionysus, and Samael are invoked to illustrate skepticism, rebellion, and the move toward an internally coherent but anti-scientific worldview.
rows.text_content: Mythic figures such as Hodur, Dionysus, and Samael are invoked to illustrate skepticism, rebellion, and the move toward an internally coherent but anti-scientific worldview.

content.free_text: book_page nonfiction_philosophy symbolism_and_archetypes Book page on symbolism, perception, and flat Earth reasoning. It argues that inner symbolic weighting can override facts and scientific order.
